additional_locations.description: Topic naming based on fork digest
Peer filtering based on fork compatibility

summary: EIP-7642 is a networking-only upgrade that does not affect fork digest; nodes without it can participate in consensus.

phase1_abstract_interpretation.summary: Analyzed fork compatibility checking mechanism in peer discovery. Fork digest comparison is independent of individual EIP implementations.

state_anomalies_found.description: EIP-7642 is labeled 'optional' but its relationship to fork digest is unclear
state_anomalies_found.abstract_state: Fork compatibility is determined solely by fork_digest, next_fork_version, and next_fork_epoch

key_observations: Fork digest (ENRForkID) encodes: fork_digest, next_fork_version, next_fork_epoch
Peer compatibility checked via isCompatibleForkId function
EIP-7642 is networking layer (eth/69) - drops pre-merge fields from protocol
Fork digest is derived from consensus fork version and genesis validators root
Networking protocol version is negotiated separately from fork compatibility

phase2_symbolic_execution.summary: No counterexample found. EIP-7642 modifies eth protocol message formats but does not affect fork digest computation.

path1.description: Node without EIP-7642 attempts peer discovery after Pectra fork
path1.conclusion: Node can discover peers regardless of EIP-7642 implementation

path2.description: Node without EIP-7642 receives gossip message
path2.path_condition: Topic subscription is based on fork_digest only
path2.conclusion: Gossip messaging works regardless of EIP-7642

path3.path_condition: RPC request contains eth/69-only field structure
path3.result: Protocol negotiation may fail at libp2p layer
path3.conclusion: EIP-7642 affects RPC protocol negotiation, not consensus participation

phase2_symbolic_execution.key_finding: EIP-7642 is a networking protocol change (eth/69) that drops pre-merge fields. It does NOT change the consensus fork digest. Consensus layer peer discovery and gossip messaging use fork digest only, which is unchanged by EIP-7642. Potential incompatibility exists at the execution layer RPC protocol level, but this does not partition nodes from consensus participation.

phase2_5_reachability_analysis.summary: Protocol incompatibility exists but does not affect consensus participation. Nodes without EIP-7642 can participate in consensus using CL-only communication.
phase2_5_reachability_analysis.data_flow_path: ENR exchange → fork digest comparison (isCompatibleForkId) → peer scoring → gossip topic subscription → consensus message validation

validation_layers.effectiveness: STRONG - Explicitly checks fork_digest equality, ensuring consensus compatibility
MODERATE - Prevents eth/68 <-> eth/69 RPC communication but does not affect CL pubsub
MODERATE - Prefers compatible peers but does not enforce exclusivity

phase2_5_reachability_analysis.notes: The ambiguity in EIP-7600 specification creates uncertainty, but the actual implementation shows that EIP-7642 (eth/69) is a protocol-level change that does NOT affect consensus fork digest. Consensus layer communication (peer discovery, gossip) uses fork digest exclusively, which is derived from consensus fork version. Nodes without EIP-7642 may have degraded EL-to-EL RPC functionality but retain full consensus participation capability. This is defense-in-depth rather than exploitable because: (1) Fork digest is unchanged by EIP-7642, (2) CL peers can communicate without EL protocol compatibility, (3) Multiple validation layers prevent actual network partition.

phase3_invariant_proving.summary: Invariant holds: nodes without EIP-7642 can participate in consensus. Proven via fork digest independence.

proof_structure.invariant: Nodes without EIP-7642 MUST NOT be partitioned from consensus participation
preconditions: Node has implemented Pectra consensus fork (EIP-7600)
Node has not implemented EIP-7642 networking upgrade (eth/69)
proof_by_construction: Step 1: Fork digest is computed from consensus fork version and genesis validators root (spec definition)
Step 2: EIP-7642 modifies eth RPC protocol message formats, not consensus fork version
Step 4: Gossip topics are constructed using fork_digest only (network.nim:52-94)
Step 5: Peer discovery filters by fork_digest compatibility (eth2_discovery.nim:152-153)
Conclusion: Nodes with same consensus fork version (Pectra) have identical fork digest regardless of EIP-7642 implementation

guard_conditions.enforcement: Peers with incompatible fork digest are filtered out, but EIP-7642 does not change fork digest

phase3_invariant_proving.conclusion: Invariant proven to hold. The ambiguity in EIP-7600 specification is misleading - EIP-7642 cannot cause consensus partition because it operates at a different protocol layer (eth RPC vs consensus gossip).

phase3_5_scope_filtering.recommendation: Clarify EIP-7600 specification to explicitly state that EIP-7642 (eth/69) does not affect consensus participation
phase3_5_scope_filtering.notes: This finding highlights a documentation/specification clarity issue rather than an actual security vulnerability. The code correctly implements protocol layering, separating consensus compatibility (fork digest) from execution protocol versioning (eth/68 vs eth/69). However, the EIP-7600 specification could be clearer about this distinction. Recommended actions: (1) Update EIP-7600 to explicitly state that EIP-7642 is an execution layer protocol change, (2) Add specification notes clarifying that consensus participation is independent of eth RPC protocol version, (3) Document expected behavior for mixed-version networks.
